coredata.dc:title: Extra-thin composite nanofiltration membranes tuned by γ-cyclodextrins containing amphipathic cavities for efficient separation of magnesium/lithium ions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Ying
Li, Nan
Shi, Jie
Xia, Yuanhua
Zhu, Bo
Shao, Ruiqi
Min, Chunying
Xu, Zhiwei
Deng, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Although nanofiltration (NF) membrane as a new frontier technique had made significant progress in the field of lithium extraction from salt lakes, they still suffered from the conventional permeability–selectivity trade-off effect. In this study, γ-cyclodextrins(CDs) containing amphipathic cavities and polyethyleneimine (PEI) acted as the aqueous phase monomer, which was carried out interfacial polymerization with the trimesoyl chloride (TMC) as the organic phase monomer to fabricate thin film composite (TFC) NF membranes with the extra-thin active layer. The hydrophilic surface and hydrophobic cavity of γ-CDs enhanced the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface and the water flux of the nanofiltration membrane. Moreover, the cavity size of γ-CDs also effectively screened magnesium and lithium ions to a certain extent. Under the best preparation conditions, the permeation flux of the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC nanofiltration membrane was 4.86 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1, SLi, Mg was 10.8. It was noteworthy that we used the molecular dynamics simulation method to confirm that the participation of γ-CDs led to the diffusion rate of the water phase monomers during the interfacial polymerization reaction slowed down. Thus, the thickness of the active layer of the nanofiltration membrane formed was also significantly reduced from 110.16 nm to 52.84 nm. This PEI/γ-CDs-TMC membrane provided a new strategy for preparing nanofiltration membranes with excellent overall magnesium-lithium separation performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: γ-cyclodextrin
Nanofiltration membrane
Separation Mg2+/Li+
Extra-thin active layer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface morphology and thickness of PEI/γ-CDs -TMC membrane
Surface functional groups and elements of PEI/γ-CDs -TMC membrane.
Hydrophilicity and potential of PEI/γ-CDs -TMC membrane
The influence of preparation conditions on the performance of NF membrane

Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22108203 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin ( 20JCZDJC00070 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme diagram of preparation for PEI/γ-CDs-TMC NF membrane.

Surface and Cross-section SEM images of the NF membranes. (a, c, e, g) Surface SEM images of NF-1, NF-2, NF-4 and NF-6 membrane; (b, d, f, h) Cross-section SEM images of the NF-1, NF-2, NF-4 and NF-6 membrane.
AFM height images of membrane surface (a) NF-1, (b) NF-2, (c) NF-3, (d) NF-4, (e) NF-5, (f) NF-6.
FTIR spectra of PES substrate, NF-1, NF-3and NF-6 membrane.
XPS spectra fitting images: (a-c) NF-1 membrane, (d-f) NF-3 membrane, and the columns represent the convoluted high resolution of C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively.
(a) Water contact angles (b) Surface zeta potentials of all prepared membranes.

(a) Separation performance of membranes prepared at different γ-CDs concentrations, (b) Separation performance of membranes prepared at different curing temperatures, (c) Separation performance of membranes prepared at different curing time.
(a) Separation factor SLi, Mg and permeation flux of PEI/γ-CDs-TMC with filtration time, (b) Influences of different ions on PEI/γ-CDs-TMC performance.
Simulated molecular model diagram (a) PEI/water (b) PEI/γ-CDs/water.
Schematic illustration of the separation mechanism of Mg2+and Li+ ions.
Surface roughness (Rq, Ra) of NF membranes.
Ion performance of the East Taijiner brine and simulated solution (diluted 4 times)[10,55]
Comparison with literature on separation of Mg2+/ Li+ by commercial NF membranes.

head.ce:title: Extra-thin composite nanofiltration membranes tuned by γ-cyclodextrins containing amphipathic cavities for efficient separation of magnesium/lithium ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Textile Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Textile Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Textile Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Neutron Physics, Institute of Nuclear Physics and Chemistry, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621999, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nuclear Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Neutron Physics, Institute of Nuclear Physics and Chemistry, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621999, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research School of Polymeric Materials, School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research School of Polymeric Materials, School of Materials Science & Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although nanofiltration (NF) membrane as a new frontier technique had made significant progress in the field of lithium extraction from salt lakes, they still suffered from the conventional permeability–selectivity trade-off effect. In this study, γ-cyclodextrins(CDs) containing amphipathic cavities and polyethyleneimine (PEI) acted as the aqueous phase monomer, which was carried out interfacial polymerization with the trimesoyl chloride (TMC) as the organic phase monomer to fabricate thin film composite (TFC) NF membranes with the extra-thin active layer. The hydrophilic surface and hydrophobic cavity of γ-CDs enhanced the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface and the water flux of the nanofiltration membrane. Moreover, the cavity size of γ-CDs also effectively screened magnesium and lithium ions to a certain extent. Under the best preparation conditions, the permeation flux of the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC nanofiltration membrane was 4.86 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1, SLi, Mg was 10.8. It was noteworthy that we used the molecular dynamics simulation method to confirm that the participation of γ-CDs led to the diffusion rate of the water phase monomers during the interfacial polymerization reaction slowed down. Thus, the thickness of the active layer of the nanofiltration membrane formed was also significantly reduced from 110.16 nm to 52.84 nm. This PEI/γ-CDs-TMC membrane provided a new strategy for preparing nanofiltration membranes with excellent overall magnesium-lithium separation performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, lithium was widely regarded as one of the rare elements due to its unique physical and chemical properties, which offered advantages in aerospace, nuclear energy, batteries and other fields [1,2]. The extensive application of lithium resources had led to a shortage of its resources, which further restricted its application and development. Among the variety of known technologies, it was noteworthy that lithium extraction from salt lakes had been widely studied to overcome the problem [3,4]. However, this method was also limited by the similar hydration radius of magnesium and lithium ions and the high proportion of magnesium and lithium ions. The hydration radius of Li+ and Mg2+ were 0.38 nm and 0.43 nm, respectively, and the ratio of Mg2+/ Li+ mainly was between 40 and 1837[5,6]. At present, the primary methods for extracting lithium from salt lakes included conventional techniques such as solvent extraction[7], adsorption[8], precipitation[9] and ion exchange[10]. Nevertheless, these technologies had apparent disadvantages, including low economic benefits, environmentally unfriendly and challenging to prepare [11–13].

Compared to those conventional methods, nanofiltration (NF) membrane had practical application and spacious development prospects in the field of magnesium-lithium separation, owing to lower cost, less energy utilization, minimizing ecological impact, and importantly outstanding separation efficiency for monovalent and multivalent ions [14]. As a common nanofiltration membrane, commercial polyamide (PA) nanofiltration membranes were obtained mainly by interfacial polymerization of diamines or polyamines and acid chlorides on the surface of ultrafiltration membranes[15,16]. Although these NF membranes had made significant progress, they were usually limited by the trade-off effect between permeability and selectivity [17,18]. At present, it was mainly used to incorporate hydrophilic components into the membrane to solve this problem. An et al. fabricated an anti-fouling NF membrane that was developed by putting in different contents of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to the interfacial polymerization of piperazine (PIP) and TMC. The hydrophilicity and permeation flux of the composite NF membrane had been heightened with the increase of the PVA mass fraction[19]. Zheng et al. prepared NF membrane-thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes by introducing sulfonated multi-walled carbon nanotubes (SMWCNT) into poly(piperazinamide) membranes. As the concentration increased, the water contact angle (WCA) gradually dropped, and the pure water permeability (PWP) raised significantly[20]. However, these hydrophilic components could only unilaterally increase the permeation flux of the membrane by introducing additional mass transfer channels or increasing the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface, and the retention performance of the membrane had not been further ameliorated for this reason. Therefore, it is essential to find a suitable hydrophilic component.

Cyclodextrins (CDs) were frustoconical macrocyclic molecules containing amphipathic cavities formed by a certain amount of D-(+)-glucopyranose units through α-1, 4 glycosidic bonds, which could be a kind of ideal material used to prepare nanofiltration membranes with defect-free active layers. At present, the most widely used cyclodextrins were α-, β- and γ-cyclodextrins [21,22]. All primary hydroxyl groups were located on one side of the ring constitute the main surface (narrower end) of the cyclodextrins truncated cone structure, and all secondary hydroxyl groups were located on the other side of the ring constitute the subsurface (wider end) of cyclodextrins[23–25], which caused the wider end of the cyclodextrins truncated cone structure and the outer wall hydrophilic. Therefore, they could be uniformly dispersed in the aqueous solution, avoiding the easy agglomeration problem of common nanomaterials and further increasing the membrane's water flux. In addition, the interior of the cavity of the cyclodextrins was a hydrophobic environment[26,27], which could be seen as a highway for water molecules to pass through. Moreover, it was noteworthy that the fixed-size cavity structure of cyclodextrins molecules could be regarded as a checkpoint for sieving ions or molecules whose size was larger than the diameter of the cavity of the cyclodextrin. Liu et al. used β-cyclodextrins (β-CDs) and TMC through interfacial polymerization on a merchant UF membrane to develop a hydrophilic TFC nanofiltration membrane, and the membrane exhibited effective dye/salt separation with high rejection of dye [22]. Based on hydrophobic cavities of a few angstroms in diameter, β-CDs had been studied to provide an improved polar solvent method and increase the permeability of NF membranes. Wu et al. used TMC and triethanolamine (TEOA) in the presence of β-CDs to prepare a polyester TFC nanofiltration membrane by interfacial polymerization. The developed NF membrane displayed a strengthening in the anti-fouling performance [28]. However, γ-CDs were rarely used in separation membranes, and there were no reports on the application of γ-CDs in the separation of magnesium and lithium, although its cavity diameter (0.75 ∼ 0.83 nm) was expected to perform well in the separation of magnesium and lithium ions.

In this work, we used PEI, γ-CDs and TMC to prepare a composite NF membrane with reform magnesium-lithium separation performance and water flux through interfacial polymerization. It was the first time to use γ-CDs as one of the interfacial polymerization aqueous phase monomer components. A large number of hydrophilic hydroxyl groups on the surface of cyclodextrins strengthened the hydrophilicity and water flux of the membrane surface. The cavity size of γ-CDs was expected to accurately and effectively perform magnesium and lithium ions size sieving. Besides, it could react with TMC to form a polyester structure to increase the stability of the film. In order to observe this, NF membranes were prepared by using γ-CDs with different loading ratios and PEI which were carried out interfacial polymerization of TMC on the surface of polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration membranes. Finally, an extra-thin active layer with a polyester structure and a polyamide structure was formed. We discussed γ-CDs on the retention performance, morphology, hydrophilicity, chemical structure and separation performance of magnesium and lithium of the composite NF membrane. In addition, we carried out molecular dynamics simulations on the diffusion of γ-CDs and PEI aqueous monomers during the interfacial polymerization procedure to understand the interfacial polymerization process more intuitively.

Polyethersulfone (PES) (Mw = 62,000 g/mol) was provided by Solvay Advanced Polymer (Belgium). Polyethylene glycol (Mw = 10,000 g/mol) and N, N-dimethylformamide were purchased from Tianjin Heowns Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. PEI (Mw = 70000 g/mol, 50 wt%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China) offered γ-cyclodextrins (γ-CDs, 98%) which were recrystallized from water twice and dried in vacuo at 90 °C for 24 h prior to use. TMC and n-hexane were furnished by Tianjin Heowns Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd to fabricate active layer via IP. Tianjin Kemei Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd provided MgCl2, NaCl, CaCl2 and LiCl to prepare different feed solutions. Deionized water as the solvent for salt solutions non-solvent for polymer precipitation. The feed solution is composed of LiCl and MgCl2 with a mass ratio of Mg2+ / Li+ of approximately 30, with a concentration of 2000 ppm.

PES porous membranes were manufactured via non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS) as a support layer[29–31]. The 17% by weight of PES polymer and 10% by weight of PEG as pore formers were dissolved in 73% by weight of DMF. The spatula immersed in a non-solvent gel bath was used to scrape the casting solution into a flat film after the static defoaming process. Then used deionized (DI) water rinsed the resulting membrane thoroughly. A traditional IP reaction developed the active layer. The two polytetrafluoroethylene frames were used to sandwich the PES membrane and keep the surface of the membrane revealing. The aqueous solution of 0.5 wt% PEI added with appropriate concentration γ-CDs was poured the mixed solution on the membrane and stayed for 30 min to ensure that the surface of the support layer could be thoroughly wetted. Then, used a rubber roller to eliminate the excess solution gently. The IP reaction was aroused by pouring 0.1 wt% TMC organic (hexane) solution on the membrane surface containing an aqueous solution. The overdose solution was drained after contact for 5 min. Then the sample was placed in a particular temperature oven for some time. Then the sample was kept in the deionized water for 24 h before testing. The reaction mechanism was demonstrated in Fig. 1 . The fabricated NF membranes were referred to as NF-1, NF-2, NF-3, NF-4, NF-5, NF-6 and the relevant content of γ-CDs was shown in Table 1 .

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, D8ADVANCE, Germany) was employed to investigate the chemical composition of the membrane surface. The surface zeta potential of the membrane was tested on the electrokinetic analyzer (SurPASSTM3 Anton Paar). The surface hydrophilicity of membranes was evaluated by contact angle goniometer (JC2000D1, China). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, TENSOR37, Germany) was used to identify functional groups on the surface of the membrane. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800) was used to acquire images of the active layer. Atomic force microscope (CSPM5500 series probe microscope, Benyuan, China) is used to check the surface roughness of the active layer.

The flat-sheet cross-flow tests were used to evaluate the flux and rejection of the membrane. The inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy (ICP-OES, PQ9000, Germany) was used to measure the selectivity and rejection of the mixed salt solution. So as to lessen the experimental blunders, all the flux and rejection tests were conducted under the conditions, which were 4 bar (operating pressure), 15 L⋅h−1 (feed flow velocity), and 25 ℃ (operating temperature). All membranes were stabilized under 4.5 bar with deionized water for 30 min before testing. The average result was obtained by performing all tests at least three times. The permeate flux (F, L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1) was calculated as Eq. 1:
F =
V
A × t × Δ p

Where
V
(L) and
A
(m2) were the permeate solution volume and effective area of the membrane, respectively.
t
(h) and
Δ p
(bar) were the sample collection time and transmembrane pressure, respectively. The rejection (R, %) of salt was calculated as Eq. 2:
R =
1 -
C p
C f
× 100 %

Where
C
p
and
C
f
represent the concentration in permeate solution and feed solution, respectively. To directly indicate the separation performance for Mg2+ and Li+ of NF membrane, the separation factor SLi, Mg was calculated by the Eq. 3:
S
Li , M g
=
C
Li , p
/
C
Mg , p
C
Li , f
/
C
Mg , f

In which
C
Mg,p
and
C
Li,p
were concentrations of Mg2+ and Li+ in the permeate solution, respectively.
C
Mg,f
and
C
Li,f
were concentrations of Mg2+ and Li+ in the feed solution, respectively.

We used the molecular dynamics simulation method to characterize the diffusion coefficients of the PEI/γ-CDs/water molecule system and the PEI/water molecule system to help us understand the effect of γ-CDs on PEI during the interfacial polymerization better and whether the diffusion behavior will have a negative impact, thereby further affecting the thickness change of the functional layer. Therefore, based on Materials Studio, we constructed a periodic non-unit cell model of water-phase mixed monomers. To put it simply, first, we used the amorphous cell module to construct the PEI/γ-CDs/water molecule system and the PEI/water molecule system. The non-unit cell model had three parts, including using the visualization module to build molecular models of water molecules, PEI and γ-CDs, and performing energy minimization calculations to obtain the optimal structure model. In the PEI/γ-CDs/water molecule system, the PEI/γ-CDs ratio was constructed according to γ-CDs: PEI = 1:1, and in the PEI/water molecule system the PEI/γ-CDs ratio was constructed according to γ-CDs: PEI = 0:1 as a reference sample. Secondly, we used the forcite module to perform dynamic equilibrium of the two systems, including NPT molecular dynamics simulation and NVT molecular dynamics simulation and obtained the final system confirmation through relaxation equilibrium. Finally, the diffusion behavior of polyethyleneimine in different systems was studied through the diffusion coefficient of the system and the diffusion trajectory of the molecules[32,33]. In the molecular simulation, the diffusion coefficient of the molecule could be obtained indirectly by calculating its mean square displacement, and the final diffusion coefficient could be calculated according to the Eq. 4:
D =
1 6
lim
t → ∞
d
dt
∑
i = 1
N a
〈
r i
t
-
r i
0
2
〉

where
N a
was the number of diffusing atoms a,
〈
r i
t
-
r i
0
2
〉
was the mean square displacement of the atom
r
i
t
and
r
i
0
denoted the position vector of atom
i
at the time t and the initial time 0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and thickness of PEI/γ-CDs -TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 showed the surface and cross-sectional morphology of different obtained NF membranes. Based on multiple previous studies [34,35], the PEI with a more considerable amount of amine groups could move into the TMC, which led to many uniformly distributed disc-like structures on the surface of the NF-1 membrane, as shown in Fig. 2 (a)[36,37]. Fig. 2 (c, e, g) showed the SEM images of the surface of NF-2, NF-4 and NF-6 membranes added with γ-CDs. It was noteworthy that disc-like structure was not observed on the surface of the membrane, which proved that the γ-CDs polyester selective layer polymerized steadily on the PES supporting membrane without obvious flaws. During the interfacial polymerization procedure, the rates of the chemical reaction and the diffusion were two linchpin factors influencing the morphology of the membrane surface[38]. Fig. 2 (b, d, f, h) showed the cross-sectional morphology of NF-1, NF-2, NF-4, and NF-6 membranes. It can be seen from the results of the cross-sectional topography that each composite NF film had a clearly visible active layer with different thicknesses on the surface layer. As shown in Fig. 2, the order of the thickness of the active layer on these NF films was as follows: NF-1 (110.16 nm) > NF-2 (71.90 nm) > NF-4 (61.24 nm) > NF-6 (52.84 nm). It was worth noting that as the concentration of γ-CDs increased, the thickness of the active layer of the NF membranes gradually decreased. It was mainly because the molecular weight of the γ-CDs molecule was relatively low relative to the PEI molecule, so the diffusion speed of γ-CDs was faster during the interfacial polymerization process. Moreover, the negative potential and a lot of hydroxyl groups on the γ-CDs surface and the hydrogen bond force between it and the PEI molecule would further restrict the diffusion of PEI to TMC, so the thickness of the formed functional layer gradually decreased[39,40]. Under the same conditions of other parameters, a thinner active layer was beneficial to increase the permeation flux of the NF membrane, thereby improving the efficiency of the NF membrane in the separation process. The reason was that as the thickness of the active layer grew, the NF membrane would form an enormous mass transfer resistance during the solute transfer process.

AFM was used to analyze the surface of NF membranes roughness changes after interfacial polymerization, and the scan size was 10 μm × 10 μm. As shown in Fig. 3 and Table 2 , the surface roughness of the NF-1 film was relatively lofty, and its root mean square roughness and average roughness were 69.8 nm and 52.7 nm, respectively. As the concentration of γ-CDs added increased from 0.1 wt% to 0.5 wt%, the roughness of the film surface also reduced. It was because the presence of γ-CDs hindered the diffusion of PEI into the organic phase and delayed disc-like structures in the active layer. However, when the concentration of γ-CDs increased further, the roughness of the membrane surface grew and pronounced bead, which may be caused by the cross-linking reaction between a large number of γ-CDs and TMC. The amplification in roughness would enlarge the effective surface area of the membrane surface, which was advantageous to enhancing the membrane's water penetration. According to previous research, the rate of the IP reaction affected the surface roughness of the NF film. In general, the lower the reaction rate interfacial polymerization had, the smoother the film surface was[41]. The anti-fouling performance of the membrane would also profit from the smooth surface[42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functional groups and elements of PEI/γ-CDs -TMC membrane.
ce:section.ce:para: ATR-FTIR spectroscopy was used to analyze the chemical structure of the PES UF membrane and NF membranes, as shown in Fig. 4 . Compared with PES membranes, NF-1, NF-3 and NF-6 membranes had a new absorption peak at 1650 cm−1, which could be attributed to the carbonyl group (-C = O) of the IP reaction that happened between the amide group on the surface of PEI and the acyl chloride group on the surface of TMC[43]. The spectra of NF-3 and NF-6 films showed a new absorption peak near 1030 cm−1, attributed to the C-O-C of the ester group. The apparent absorption signal of NF-3 and NF-6 membrane at 1725 cm−1 represented the carbonyl group (C = O) of the ester compound. It indicated that interfacial polymerization had occurred between the –OH group of γ-CDs and the acid chloride group of TMC[44]. The peaks at 3350 cm−1 of NF-3 and NF-6 membranes were owing to the overlap of the amine groups (-N-H) and the hydroxyl groups (-O-H) stretching vibrations. It could be related to the surfeit of γ-CDs. Moreover, the absorbance peaks were strengthened with the growth of γ-CDs density.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the carbon spectra of PES/PEI NF film and PES/PEI/γ-CDs NF film could be decomposed into C-C, C-N, C-O, N-C = O and O-C = O components, and the blinding energy were approximately 284.8, 285.7, 286.3, 287.7, and 288.9 eV, while traditional PES/PA NF membranes only contain the first four carbon substances. In addition, three nitrogen substances were corresponding to 399.1, 400.0 and 401.3 eV, corresponding to C-NH2, N-C = O and NHn +(n = 2, 3), respectively[41,45,46]. With the addition of the γ-CDs, the content of N-C = O in the carbon species dropped. Besides, a small amount of O-C = O appeared on the surface of the PES/PEI/γ-CDs NF membrane, -O-C = O also demonstrated that γ-CDs and TMC successfully polymerized to form a selective layer through the IP reaction. This change proved that the polymerization of γ-CDs and TMC inhibited the formation of polyester structure to a certain extent. The higher the carboxyl content, the more pronounced the effect. From the perspective of nitrogen species, the content of –NHn + (n = 2, 3) increased from 13.84% of the traditional PEI-TMC NF membrane to the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC NF membrane 21.56%. The –NHn +(n = 2, 3) group peak at 401.3 eV was a characteristic peak of the –NH3 + or –NH2 + due to the protonation of –NH or unreacted –NH2 in water. The surface was positively charged and could also be proved by the zeta potential characterization results in Fig. 6 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrophilicity and potential of PEI/γ-CDs -TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further study the film surface characteristics, we further tested the static contact angle and zeta potential of the film surface. Contact angle measurement was used to characterize the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface. In general, the membrane with better surface hydrophilicity had a smaller contact value. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the static contact angle of the PEI-TMC film was 61°, which was similar to the results in the literature[14]. As expected, adding γ-CDs made the hydrophilicity of the NF membrane surface heighten. As the concentration of γ-CDs increased, the contact angle of the membrane surface dropped below 45°, which was reasonably attributed to the internal hydrophobic cavity structure of the γ-CDs molecule and the stronger external hydrophilicity that could effectively promote droplet diffusion[47]. This was also one of the main reasons for the high permeability of PEI/γ-CDs-TMC membranes. In general, as one of the components of the aqueous solution, γ-CDs effectively heightened the hydrophilicity of the active layer of the NF membrane and increased its affinity for water molecules.

The surface charge features of the membrane certainly affected the retention and absorption of charged species in the solution[48,49]. Therefore, the zeta potential of all membranes had been studied within a specific pH range (3.0–10.0), and the test results were shown in Fig. 6 (b). In addition to stronger hydrophilicity, the surface potential of the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC membrane showed slightly higher than the positive potential of the PEI-TMC membrane. Moreover, the positive potential on the surface of the membrane strengthened as the content of γ-CDs increased. The changeability of the composite NF membrane surface was mainly caused by the protonation of amine groups on the surface of the NF membrane and the dissociation of carboxyl groups[14,50]. When the pH of the solution was higher than the isoelectric point, the surface of the NF membrane was positively charged, which was attributed to the protonation of unreacted amine functional groups on the surface of the NF membrane. In contrast, the deprotonation of the carboxyl NF membrane made the membrane surface negatively charged when the pH of the solution was below the isoelectric point. The diffusion speed of γ-CDs relative to PEI was faster. After the reaction of γ-CDs and TMC, the diffusion of PEI to TMC was further restricted, so many unreacted amine groups on the surface of the membrane made the film surface show a positive potential. When the active layer was sufficiently dense, the separation performance of the composite NF membrane for Mg2+ and Li+ was mainly affected by the electrostatic effect. However, if the resulting active layer was relatively flabby, the separation performance of the composite NF membrane for Mg2+ and Li+ was mainly affected by steric hindrance, which will reduce the selective separation of Mg2+ and Li+ of the NF membrane[51,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of preparation conditions on the performance of NF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to numerous amine and hydroxyl groups in PEI and γ-CDs, respectively, they were expected to polymerize with TMC to form an active layer. The separation performance of the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC NF membrane was mainly determined by the property of the active layer obtained through IP. Accordingly, we researched the effect of every single parameter to investigate the optimal condition.

When the concentration of γ-CDs was 0 wt%, the permeation flux of the nanofiltration membrane was 3.15 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1, and the value of SLi, Mg was 7.15. The cavity structure of the introduced γ-CDs could provide many channels for water molecules in the transmission of the active layer, resulting in increased water flux, so as the concentration of γ-CDs increased from 0.1 wt% to 0.9 wt%, the permeation flux raised from 3.51 to 13.08 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1. Furthermore, γ-CDs hindered the diffusion of PEI into the organic phase in the IP reaction process, which influenced the constitution of the structure in the active layer. It was the reason that the water transfer distance was shortened to a certain extent, and the water mass transfer resistance was reduced. On the other hand, the cavity size of γ-CDs was between the hydration radius of magnesium and lithium ions[53], so the size of magnesium and lithium ions can be effectively sieved. Therefore, when the content of γ-CDs expanded from 0.1 wt% to 0.3 wt%, SLi, Mg raised from 7.52 to 10.8. However, when the mass fraction of γ-CDs in the aqueous monomer continues to increase, SLi, Mg dropped from 7.39 to 1.25. Under the synergy of Donnan repulsion and steric hindrance, the excessive aggregation of γ-CDs may cause this phenomenon. The results showed that the optimal concentration of γ-CDs in this study was 0.3 wt%.

In addition, we designed different curing parameters, including curing temperature and curing time, to investigate their effect on the separation performance of the nanofiltration membranes. Under the conditions of 0.5 wt% PEI, 0.3 wt% γ-CDs aqueous solutions, 0.1 wt% TMC n-hexane solution and 15 min curing time, the influence of curing temperature on the separation performance is shown in Fig. 7 (b). In general, curing temperature was one of the most critical elements affecting membrane separation performance. We found that the separation factors SLi, Mg increased from 1.14 to 10.8 when the curing temperature was 50 °C to 60 °C, while the permeation flux reduced by 76.12%. This result may be due to the heat treatment temperature, which can facilitate polymerization of PEI, γ-CDs and TMC. Subsequently, the high heat treatment temperature would arouse the thin active layer to shrink, which meant the SLi, Mg would be significantly and negatively affected. The results showed that the best heat treatment temperature was 60 °C.

In addition, in Fig. 7 (c), we also studied the effect of curing time on separation performance. As we can see, the heat treatment time was increased from 5 min to 15 min, and the polyamide functional film was gradually optimized. The separation coefficient of NF membrane, SLi, Mg increased from 0.98 to 10.8, and the rejection rate of Mg2+ by the membrane was significantly increased from 2.30% to 96%. At this time, continuing to increase the heat treatment time would be detrimental to the separation performance and permeability of the final NF membrane. Although the interception rate increased from 38.78% to 68.88%, the water flux dropped from 15.2 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1 to 4.86 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1. A possible reason may be that when the heat treatment time for the film was too short, there would not be enough time for the polymerization reaction to arise, so the structural properties of the active layer were poor. As the heat treatment time increased, the degree of polymerization between PEI, γ-CDs and TMC increased, and the cross-linking rate increased, resulting in a dense functional thin layer on the PES base film. Therefore, the selective separation performance of the NF membrane pair gradually ameliorated while the corresponding water flux decreased. The results showed that the optimal curing time of the composite NF film was 15 min.

In summary, so as to improve the selective separation of Mg2+ and Li+ by the NF membrane, the best interfacial polymerization conditions were that the PES base membrane was immersed in a 0.5 wt% PEI, 0.3 wt% γ-CDs aqueous solution for 30 min, and then immersed in 0.1 wt% TMC n-hexane solution for 5 min, then heated treatment at 60 °C for 15 min.

A long-term test was applied to analyze the separation and permeation flux stability of the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC membrane, and the results were demonstrated in Fig. 8
(a). The long-term filterability test results of the membrane showed that the SLi, Mg of the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC did not fluctuate much during the 120-hour long-term operation, but the flux of PEI/γ-CDs-TMC was slightly reduced due to the membrane fouling phenomenon. These results showed that the membrane had stable selectivity in long-term processes.

In order to be closer to the practical application, the simulated brine (as shown in Table 3 ) was used as the feed solution for separation, and the results were shown in Fig. 8 (b). In industry, natural saline water or seawater needs a series of pretreatment to obtain brine which can be used as feed solution for the separation of Mg2+/Li+ by NF membranes such as precipitation, adsorption and dilution. Before brine is used as the feed solution, the K+ ions have been extracted. Therefore, the simulated solution does not contain K+ ions. In the separation experiment, we used simulated salt lake brine as the raw material liquid. The viscosity of the raw material liquid was also increased relative to that of the mixed salt solution because the concentration of the raw material liquid was high[54]. Therefore, the flux of the membrane also dropped from 4.86 to 1.19 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1, but overall, the rejection of the membrane to Li+ and Na+ was still lower than that of Mg2+ and Ca2+. In addition, due to the concentration polarization that may occur in high-concentration solutions, the rejection rate of Mg2+ and Li+ of PEI/γ-CDs-TMC was significantly reduced.

In order to further understand the separation mechanism of the prepared NF membrane, Table 4 summarized the separation performance of the commercial nanofiltration membrane reported in the literature and the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC membrane prepared by us on Mg2+/ Li+. Compared with these nanofiltration membranes, the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC membrane had excellent magnesium-lithium separation performance and permeation flux. Generally speaking, polymer nanofiltration membranes had lower permeation flux and higher Mg2+/ Li+ separation performance, resulting from the typical “trade-off” phenomenon between selectivity and permeability[56]. Although the permeation flux of the nanofiltration membrane was significantly increased after the addition of cyclodextrins, the separation performance in some kinds of literature was significantly reduced, which was still limited by “choices”[57]. In this study, to a certain extent, higher water flux and higher separation factors Sli, Mg were obtained simultaneously, reached 4.86 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1 and 10.8.

The PEI/γ-CDs-TMC nanofiltration membrane exhibited high permeability and excellent separation performance due to its ultra-thin active layer (52.84 nm), sizeable unreacted amine groups and hydrophilic surface structure. Its schematic diagram was shown in Fig. 10 . The interpretation of high permeability could be eventuated as the following three aspects. Above all, the addition of γ-CDs was favorable to reduce the rate of IP, resulting in a loose ultra-thin structure. This result could be confirmed from our molecular simulation experiments. As shown in Fig. 9, after the participating of γ-CDs molecules, the diffusion coefficient of the aqueous monomer was reduced from 7.48 to 5.30.

Secondly, the attending of γ-CDs grew in number the water channels, and its internal cavity was hydrophobic, allowing water molecules to pass more quickly. Last but not least, compared with the PEI-TMC membrane, the hydrophilicity of the surface of the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC membrane was extensively heightened, and the resistance of water molecules through hydrophilic channels was lower. Generally speaking, a higher positive potential on the membrane surface was conducive to obtain excellent separation efficiency. As far as we know, owing to the higher positive potential on the surface of the PEI/γ-CDs-TMC membrane had, the charge repulsion of the membrane to Mg2+ ions was more vital than that of Li+ ions. Therefore, the concentration of Li+ ions on the surface of the membrane particles was higher than that of Mg2+ ions, and Li+ ions had more opportunities to enter the nanopores, which followed the typical Donnan repulsion effect[62]. In addition, a non-uniform electric double layer (stem layer and diffusion layer) was formed on the surface of the positively charged membrane. The weak layer facilitated the passage of a large amount of Li+ ions and a small amount of Mg2+ ions. The results demonstrated that the ion mass transfer resistance decreased, and the ion transmission rate increased, weakened the concentration polarization effect.

In summary, we developed a new nanofiltration membrane with an extra-thin active layer (52.84 nm) with cavity structure. Benefitting from the partaking of γ-CDs, which had a great quantity of hydroxyl groups on its surface, slowed down the diffusion rate of the monomer in the water phase, so the progress of the interfacial polymerization reaction was decelerated ulteriorly. Moreover, γ-CDs enhanced the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface. The water contact angle reduced from 61 to 44.5° could prove this point. The hydrophobicity cavity of the γ-CDs accelerated the rapid transmission of water molecules, and the size of the cavity between magnesium and lithium ions further enhanced the separation performance of the nanofiltration membrane. Under the best preparation conditions, the permeation flux was 4.86 L⋅m−2⋅h−1⋅bar−1 and the separation coefficient was 10.8 of the NF membrane. The results of zeta potential of NF membrane surface also exhibited positive charges, ascribed to the fact that γ-CDs partly limited the spread of PEI to TMC. These results jointly indicated that the NF membranes had a small water transmission distance, excellent hydrophilicity, and a positive potential on the surface of the membrane, which was beneficial to upgrade the magnesium-lithium separation performance of the nanofiltration membrane and furnish a new way to reclaim lithium from salt lakes.

Ying Zhao: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Nan Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Jie Shi: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Yuanhua Xia: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Bo Zhu: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ruiqi Shao: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Chunying Min: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation. Zhiwei Xu: Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Hui Deng: Resources, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22108203) and the Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (20JCZDJC00070)
